initial_posts.narrative: As an AI engineer in a health-tech startup, I see the government's commitment to AI R&D as substantial but practically constrained. The policy states over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore, so I take that as the total funding. The co-funding cap of S$330,000 per 100E project is explicitly stated. For compute, I estimate 500,000 GPU hours based on typical allocations for national AI programs of this scale, considering that most compute goes to industry use and my startup's experience with cloud credits. Trainers: I estimate 50 personnel, drawing from my knowledge of the AI Apprenticeship Programme and other upskilling initiatives, which require a dedicated but not massive team of experts to guide enterprises and practitioners.
evidence: The policy context explicitly states 'Over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D' and 'co-funding up to S$330,000 per project for 100 Experiments', so I take these as ground truth.

For GPU hours, I reason from the policy's emphasis on compute infrastructure for industry and my own experience: a national program like NAIS 2.0 would allocate hundreds of thousands of hours, not millions, given Singapore's size and the need to balance across many use cases.

Trainer count is based on the scale of the AI Apprenticeship Programme and other training efforts: a lean team of around 50 AI experts can effectively mentor enterprises and run workshops, as seen in similar programs I've interacted with.

Platform onboarding assists will be around 200, reflecting the need to help enterprises and researchers get onto the AI experimentation platform, with each assist being a technical session. initial_posts.narrative: Given the S$500 million total funding, S$330,000 co-funding cap, 500,000 GPU hours, and 50 trainers, I estimate 80 approved 100E projects in 2023. This is based on my direct experience with the programme: the application and review process is rigorous, and not all proposals meet the 'no commercial off-the-shelf solution' criterion. The 100 outreach sessions and 25 matchmaking events likely generated around 120-150 proposals, but only about 60-70% get approved due to technical feasibility and business impact. For AI prototypes, I estimate 60 built, as some approved projects may stall or pivot during development—my own AML prototype took several iterations. Trainees completing the programme: with 35 workshops and 150 onboarding assists, plus the 9-month AIAP structure, I estimate 120 trainees finish in 2023, assuming cohorts of ~40-50 per intake. Enterprises with at least one solution: 70, as some enterprises may have multiple projects but others drop out. These numbers are lower than some might expect because I've seen firsthand the challenges of translating approvals into working prototypes and trained talent.
evidence: My direct participation in the 100E programme showed that the approval process is selective—only about 60-70% of well-prepared proposals get funded, so 80 approvals from ~120 proposals is realistic.

From my AML prototype experience, not all approved projects result in a working prototype due to technical hurdles or shifting priorities; 60 prototypes from 80 approvals reflects a 75% success rate, which is typical in my experience.

The AIAP is a 9-month programme, so with 50 trainers and 35 workshops, the maximum throughput is limited; 120 completions in 2023 aligns with a steady-state of ~40 trainees per cohort across three intakes.
